title: ISE Sponsor Portal — Sponsor Approval Latency and Approval-Pattern Anomalies
dataSources: ISE syslog `index=nac sourcetype=cisco:ise:syslog` *Sponsor Portal* category — codes `86001` (sponsor login), `86002` (guest account created), `86003` (guest account approved), `86004` (guest account rejected), `86005` (guest account denied — duplicate), `86010` (sponsor portal latency anomaly), `86020` (mass-approval action). Fields: `sponsor_user`, `guest_user`, `guest_count`, `latency_ms`. CIM mapping: `Authentication.action`.
spl:
index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:syslog" MessageCode IN (86002, 86003, 86004, 86020)
| eval action = case(MessageCode=="86002", "created", MessageCode=="86003", "approved", MessageCode=="86004", "rejected", MessageCode=="86020", "mass_approval")
| stats count, dc(guest_user) as unique_guests, max(guest_count) as max_batch by sponsor_user, action
| where (action=="approved" AND count > 50) OR action=="mass_approval" OR (action=="created" AND count > 100)
description: Audits guest-account sponsor activity and detects mass-approval patterns suggesting either a legitimate event (conference, large meeting) or a compromised sponsor account approving thousands of guests for malicious purposes.
value: Sponsor accounts have authority to onboard guests onto the network. A compromised sponsor account can be used to onboard attacker-controlled devices. Mass-approval pattern detection within 5 minutes is the primary control for this risk.
implementation: Confirm *Sponsor Portal* category at INFO. Schedule every 5 min, search window `-15m@m to @m`, trigger on count > 0, throttle suppression on `sponsor_user`+`action` for 30 min.
visualization: (1) Top-N: sponsors by approval volume last 7d. (2) Trend: approvals per day. (3) Anomaly panel: sponsors whose approval rate is >3 sigma above their personal baseline.

references.title: Cisco ISE 3.4 Admin Guide — Guest and Sponsor Portals

knownFalsePositives: (a) **Conference / event onboarding** — Legitimate mass-approval for a 500-person event. Cross-reference event calendar. (b) **Daily approval cadence** — Some sponsors regularly approve 30+ guests/day; build a per-sponsor baseline and only alert on anomalies relative to baseline.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(d) — guest access is part of access control.
compliance.controlObjective: Sponsored guest-access lifecycle audit and approval-trail evidence.

compliance.assurance_rationale: AC-2 (Account Management) — sponsor approvals create accounts.
A.5.16 (Identity management) — guest identity creation audit.
PCI 8.2.4 — addition/modification of user identifiers must be authorised.

controlTest.positiveScenario: From the ISE sponsor portal, mass-approve 51 guest accounts. ISE emits 86003 events; UC-17.1.51 fires showing the sponsor with `action=approved, count=51`.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Approve 10 accounts. UC-17.1.51 records but does not fire.

detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• ISE sponsor portal in use.
• `sponsor_baseline.csv` lookup with `sponsor_user`, `avg_daily_approvals`, `stdev_daily_approvals`.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Enable *Sponsor Portal* category at INFO.

Step 2 — Create the search
Search in `spl` field. Schedule every 5 min.

A secondary anomaly search compares per-sponsor activity to baseline:
```spl
index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:syslog" MessageCode=86003 earliest=-1d
| stats count by sponsor_user
| lookup sponsor_baseline.csv sponsor_user OUTPUT avg_daily_approvals, stdev_daily_approvals
| eval z = (count - avg_daily_approvals) / stdev_daily_approvals
| where z > 3
```

Step 3 — Validate
Provoke as above.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard "ISE — Sponsor Activity":
• Top-N sponsors by approval volume.
• Trend: approvals per day.
• Anomaly panel: sponsors above baseline.

Alerting: PagerDuty medium on sponsor anomaly.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **No 86003 events** — *Sponsor Portal* category at WARN.
• **`sponsor_user` empty** — TA mis-extraction.
• **Baseline empty** — Nightly summary search to build baseline failed.
grandmaExplanation: When an employee creates guest accounts for visitors, we count them. If one employee suddenly creates dozens or hundreds of guest accounts, we ring the bell — it might be a real conference, or it might be someone abusing their access.
